name: Preference Retention
description: Tests whether memorized code preferences are applied without re-stating them. With brain memory, the agent should automatically use the preferred patterns (functional style, immutable data, specific naming) without being reminded.

memories.title: Functional Programming Preferences
memories.body: Prefer functional style: pure functions, immutable data, map/filter/reduce over for loops. No classes — use factory functions and closures instead. Use const exclusively (never let or var). Prefer Object.freeze for config objects.

memories.title: Naming and Documentation Preferences
memories.body: Descriptive function names that read as verbs: getUserById, parseConfig, validateEmail. No abbreviations except well-known ones (id, url, api). JSDoc only for exported functions. No inline comments unless the logic is genuinely non-obvious.
memories.content:
# Naming and Documentation Preferences

## Naming
- Functions: verb phrases — `getUserById`, `parseConfig`, `validateEmail`
- Constants: UPPER_SNAKE — `MAX_RETRIES`, `DEFAULT_PORT`
- Booleans: is/has/can prefix — `isValid`, `hasPermission`, `canEdit`
- No abbreviations except: id, url, api, db, config, env

## Documentation
- JSDoc only for exported public functions
- No inline comments unless logic is non-obvious
- Prefer self-documenting code over comments
- README for module-level documentation

memories.title: Error Handling Preferences
memories.body: Use Result pattern instead of try/catch: functions return { ok: true, data } or { ok: false, error }. Caller handles the result explicitly. Only use try/catch at boundaries (HTTP handlers, file I/O). Never swallow errors silently.

test.label: create-data-pipeline
test.text: Create a data transformation pipeline module (pipeline.js) that: 1) Reads a list of raw user records, 2) Validates each record, 3) Transforms valid records (normalize names, format emails), 4) Filters out invalid ones, 5) Returns a summary with counts. Just implement it using clean, idiomatic JavaScript following our project's established conventions.
